notes: Test prompts exercising different diagram archetypes. Each prompt has expectations for what the resulting diagram should and should not contain. These have not been run through a formal benchmark; they are seed cases for users running the skill-creator evaluation loop.

evals.name: request-trace-multi-tenant-rag
evals.prompt: Draw a system diagram for a request flowing through a multi-tenant retrieval-augmented generation service. The platform has an API gateway that does PII redaction, a shared orchestrator with plan/execute/verify/adjust stages, an LLM router with cost tiers, retriever adapters that hit a tenant-scoped vector store, an eval harness, a feedback pipeline that updates a skill registry, and a per-tenant hash-chained audit log.
expected_to_include: A plan section in plain text before the Mermaid source
Three subgraphs encoding trust boundary (tenant-scoped, shared platform, per-tenant storage)
A single trace from the API client back to the API client
Audit log as a single sink with multiple dotted incoming arrows
PII redaction as an edge label, not a separate box
Plan-execute-verify-adjust loop drawn as a real loop (verify can return to plan via adjust)
expected_to_exclude: Failure recovery / circuit breakers as peer flow
More than one cadence (no batch jobs alongside the request trace)
Decorative color (every color must map to the named semantic axis)

evals.name: ci-cd-pipeline
evals.prompt: Draw a diagram of how a pull request flows through a typical CI/CD pipeline: developer pushes to a feature branch, GitHub Actions runs lint and unit tests, on green the PR can be reviewed, after approval it merges to main, which triggers a build/push to a container registry, then ArgoCD detects the new image and deploys to staging, then a manual gate promotes to prod.
expected_to_include: Plan section naming the trace from developer push to prod deploy
Linear flow with a clear branch for the manual gate to prod
Stages as nodes, transitions as labeled edges
Some semantic distinction between automated stages and human-gated stages
expected_to_exclude: Trust-boundary subgraphs (not the right axis here)
Failure paths (test failures, deploy rollbacks) as peer flows
Inventory of every CI step or deploy-tool config detail

evals.name: iteration-mode-redraw
evals.archetype: diagnostic mode on existing messy diagram
evals.prompt: Here's a diagram of our checkout flow [user attaches a screenshot or describes a layered diagram with 12+ boxes, decorative color, audit logging drawn as a peer service, and missing return arrows]. It's hard to follow — can you redraw it?
expected_to_include: Diagnosis BEFORE redrawing: 3-4 named failure modes from the diagnostic checklist
Explicit statement of which structural problem is most load-bearing
Plan section naming the trace before producing Mermaid
Redrawn diagram following the standard procedure
expected_to_exclude: Going straight to redraw without diagnosis
Listing every flaw rather than the 3-4 highest-leverage ones
Reproducing the original's failure modes in the redraw

evals.name: out-of-scope-erd
evals.archetype: skill should NOT trigger / should redirect
evals.prompt: Draw me an ER diagram of user, post, and comment tables in a typical blog schema.
expected_to_include: Recognition that this is an ERD, not a system architecture diagram
Direct use of Mermaid erDiagram syntax (or recommendation thereof)
Either no invocation of the skill, or the skill recognizing the case and stepping aside
expected_to_exclude: Forcing tables into a flowchart with subgraphs
Encoding columns as edges or trust boundaries
Going through the trace-planning procedure for what is not a trace

evals.name: ml-training-pipeline
evals.archetype: cross-cadence loop, not a request trace
evals.prompt: Draw a diagram of a typical ML training pipeline: a nightly job pulls labeled data from a data warehouse, a feature engineering step writes to a feature store, the trainer reads features and labels, runs distributed training on a GPU cluster, writes checkpoints to object storage, an eval job loads the checkpoint and runs a holdout suite, on pass the model gets registered in the model registry, and the serving infra picks up the new version.
expected_to_include: Recognition that this is a batch pipeline, not a request trace
Plan section that names the cadence (nightly) explicitly
Linear flow with a clear gate at the eval step
Storage components (warehouse, feature store, object storage, registry) drawn as cylinders or sinks
expected_to_exclude: Forcing a 'user' or 'request' entry point that doesn't exist in this pipeline
Real-time / synchronous serving path mixed in (different cadence)
Inventory of every dataset, model artifact, or job-runner detail
